title: Friday Feedback 03/07
summary:
If you’ve been reading the news and wondering what your peers think of it, look no further. Our Friday Feedback segment provides a platform for the voices of our readers, highlighting the thoughts, reflections and musings of members of the wine industry. Read our recent roundup including a response to our ‘New Wine Australia investment agreement secures AWRI research and industry services to 2030’ story.

The post Friday Feedback 03/07 appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
If you’ve been reading the news and wondering what your peers think of it, look no further.

Our Friday Feedback segment provides a platform for the voices of our readers, highlighting the thoughts, reflections and musings of members of the wine industry.

 

Read our recent roundup here:

 

Responding to New Wine Australia investment agreement secures AWRI research and industry services to 2030

“The AWRI has been exemplary in the manner it has conducted itself for the entire time of my winemaking journey on the Granite Belt – 42yrs. The high level of research and implementation is to be praised. The staffing has been excellent. This is a body which has over and over demonstrated innovation and support for the wine industry. The devising of the GG Assay and the Closure trials are two examples that spring to mind. The MRL data base has been a pivotal resource base.

Well done.”

  • Tony Comino

 

Responding to Grillo

“Thanks for all the great info. we just bottled out first Grillo and it’s interesting to get others’ experience.”

  • Jefferson A Hinchliffe

The post Friday Feedback 03/07 appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Central Otago’s Young Winemaker and Young Viticulturist of the Year winners announced
summary:
The winners for Central Otago’s Young Winemaker and Young Viticulturist competition were announced yesterday at a combined awards dinner held at Bannockburn Hall. Lisa Fromont from Te Kano won the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey Central Otago Young Winemaker of the Year award whilst Matthew Kerruish from Vinewise Viticulture took home the 2026 Ormond Nurseries Central Otago Young Viticulturist of the Year title. Fromont is primarily based at Te Kano’s Waitaki Estate, where she is 2IC in both the vineyards and the winery. Kerruish works for Vinewise as well as at his family vineyards and winery Folding Hills Wines in Bendigo. At the same award night, Joshua Irving from Lincoln University became the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey North Canterbury Young Winemaker of the Year.

The post Central Otago’s Young Winemaker and Young Viticulturist of the Year winners announced appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The Viticulturist of the Year contestants. Images supplied

The winners for Central Otago’s Young Winemaker and Young Viticulturist competition were announced yesterday at a combined awards dinner held at Bannockburn Hall.

Lisa Fromont from Te Kano won the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey Central Otago Young Winemaker of the Year award whilst Matthew Kerruish from Vinewise Viticulture took home the 2026 Ormond Nurseries Central Otago Young Viticulturist of the Year title.

Fromont is primarily based at Te Kano’s Waitaki Estate, where she is 2IC in both the vineyards and the winery. Kerruish works for Vinewise as well as at his family vineyards and winery Folding Hills Wines in Bendigo.

At the same award night, Joshua Irving from Lincoln University became the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey North Canterbury Young Winemaker of the Year.

Irving also recently became this year’s North Canterbury Young Viticulturist of the Year. Due to winning the title for each competition, he will be the first person ever to compete in both the Young Viticulturist and Young Winemaker national finals in the same year.

“I know there’s a lot of hard work ahead, but I see it as a great opportunity and am excited to seize it,” he said.

Young Winemaker contestants

Congratulations also go to Matt O’Connor from Prophet’s Rock who came third in the Young Winemaker competition.

Rachel Tonk won the Constellation Brands Laboratory Section and Fromont took out the Fruitfed Supplies Best Speech and the Indevin Wine Judging section.

The other contestants were Lachie John, Mount Edward; Maggie Wise, VinPro; Matthew Kerruish, Matt Connell Wines; and Regina Marks, Monte Christo.

Young Viticulturist contestants

The Young Viticulturist competition was held at Otago Polytechnic’s Central Campus on Thursday.

New Zealand Winegrowers congratulated runner ups Braxton Benseman from Mt Difficulty who came second and to Chavi Cleworth from Carrick who came third.

Cleworth also won the BioStart Hortisports whilst Benseman came first in the Syngenta Biologicals Nutrition Section. Jack McMonagle took out the head-to-head Ecotrellis section.

Savannah McGhie from Burn Cottage was awarded the Ormond Nurseries Professional Reputation Award for her “positive, friendly attitude as well as huge growth over the last year”.

The other contestants included Olive Roberts (Peregrine), Abby Crawford and Hamish Anderson – both from Felton Road.

The National Finals

Matthew Kerruish is reportedly thrilled to go through to the national final and is “determined to do Central Otago proud” as he competes for the overall title of 2026 Ormond Nurseries Young Viticulturist of the Year, being held in August.

Lisa Fromont will be on the Burgundy Exchange, however, that experience has a date clash with the national finals, also being held in August. This means she will not be representing Central Otago at the national finals of the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey Young Winemaker of the Year.

The regional chairs were Annabel Bulk and Larissa Woods from Felton Road. Having both competed previously, New Zealand Winegrowers said the two “enjoy giving back and helping the next generation come through”.

title: ‘Beyond the Bottle’ trade event set for Melbourne
summary:
Alepat Taylor will host a dedicated wine and hospitality trade day in Melbourne at Rydges on 27 July. The event, titled ‘Beyond the Bottle 2026, is designed to “educate, inspire and connect the industry through world-class producers, premium food and cutting-edge hospitality solutions”. Open exclusively to trade and free to attend, the event will bring together over 35 international and domestic producers. Organisers claim the event presents a “rare opportunity for sommeliers, buyers and operators to engage with the people behind the wines and deepen their understanding of regional styles, philosophy and production”.

The post ‘Beyond the Bottle’ trade event set for Melbourne appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The event aims to bring together sommeliers, buyers and operators from around the world. Image supplied

Alepat Taylor will host a dedicated wine and hospitality trade day in Melbourne at Rydges on 27 July.

The event, titled ‘Beyond the Bottle 2026, is designed to “educate, inspire and connect the industry through world-class producers, premium food and cutting-edge hospitality solutions”.

Open exclusively to trade and free to attend, the event will bring together over 35 international and domestic producers. Organisers claim the event presents a “rare opportunity for sommeliers, buyers and operators to engage with the people behind the wines and deepen their understanding of regional styles, philosophy and production”.

Alepat Taylor said that a key focus for this year’s event is “education through exploration”, with a “standout” Italian offering that it believes showcases the country in its entirety and therefore helps attendees build “a comprehensive understanding of one of the world’s most diverse wine landscapes”.

The event will also highlight Australia’s own wine landscape with a selection from South Australia’s Barossa Valley and Clare Valley through to Victoria’s Yarra Valley.

Bruno 1956 (formerly Bruno Fine Foods) will present a range of premium Italian gourmet products while Cedar Hospitality displays a range of catering equipment and service solutions.

The event will host three “exclusive VIP masterclasses, providing focused deep dives into key themes and categories”:

  1. Cordero di Montezemolo |

Barolo vertical tasting with Alberto Cordero di Montezemolo

  1. New Custodians of the Barossa

Discussion on the future of the Barossa with Sophie Melton of Charles Melton Wines, Adam Fiegert of Gibson and Damien Tscharke of Tscharke Wines

  1. L’Art de l’Expression

Maxence Goinere from Camus Cognac will delve into the terroir of Cognac and the blending experience

These sessions are designed for trade professionals “seeking deeper, actionable knowledge, with insights delivered directly by producers and industry experts”.

title: California Wines partners with Sydney steakhouse for July-long wine showcase
summary:
California Wines has partnered with Sydney steakhouse The Gidley to present a month-long showcase “celebrating the diversity and quality” of California’s wine regions. The showcase has been designed to introduce Sydney wine lovers to the breadth of styles, appellations and varietals produced across the Golden State. California Wines’ joint regional director for North Asia and Australasia Hiro Tejima said that Australian wine drinkers may only know California for Cabernet Sauvignon expressions – however, he believes that’s only a fraction of what it has to offer. "Australian wine drinkers may know California for certain expressions of Cabernet Sauvignon, but that's only a fraction of what the Golden State has to offer. California is one of the most dynamic wine producing regions in the world, with everything from cool-climate Chardonnay and Pinot Noir to age-worthy reds including Cinsault and Zinfandel from historic old-vine vineyards.”

The post California Wines partners with Sydney steakhouse for July-long wine showcase appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The wines have been curated by Emma Chappell, head sommelier at The Gidley’s. Image credit Dominic Loneragan

California Wines has partnered with Sydney steakhouse The Gidley to present a month-long showcase “celebrating the diversity and quality” of California’s wine regions.

The showcase has been designed to introduce Sydney wine lovers to the breadth of styles, appellations and varietals produced across the Golden State.

California Wines’ joint regional director for North Asia and Australasia Hiro Tejima said that Australian wine drinkers may only know California for Cabernet Sauvignon expressions – however, he believes that’s only a fraction of what it has to offer.

“Australian wine drinkers may know California for certain expressions of Cabernet Sauvignon, but that’s only a fraction of what the Golden State has to offer. California is one of the most dynamic wine producing regions in the world, with everything from cool-climate Chardonnay and Pinot Noir to age-worthy reds including Cinsault and Zinfandel from historic old-vine vineyards.

“As Americans prepare to celebrate Independence Day and the 250th anniversary of the United States, this partnership with The Gidley provides a fitting opportunity for Sydneysiders to discover a wider range of Californian wine through a carefully curated selection of producers and regions,” he said.

Launching on 3 July, the showcase has been curated by The Gidley’s head sommelier Emma Chappel and features 31 Californian wines available by the bottle, with seven new wines not already on The Gidley menu being exclusively available for the duration of the showcase.

When curating the list, Chappell said she wanted to showcase both the wines people expect from California and some they may have never encountered before.

Across the restaurant and lounge, the venue will also offer a California by-the-glass program featuring seven wines, “allowing guests to explore a range of regions and styles without committing to a full bottle”.

“Of course, there are iconic Cabernet Sauvs and Chardonnays, but there are also varieties like Trousseau and Syrah, even sparkling, that demonstrate just how diverse and exciting California has become. The by-the-glass program allows guests to explore multiple regions and styles in a single visit, which is something very few wine lists can offer,” she shared.

The wine menu is subject to demand and will “evolve throughout the month” with rotating selections.

Best known for regions like the Napa Valley and Sonoma County, California produces more than 80% of all American wine and is home to “a diverse collection of coastal, mountain and inland wine regions”.

The restaurant will also offer an “exclusive” Pastrami Beef Rib with Cabernet Glaze and Pickled Cucumber for the duration of the wine promotion.

title: Orange’s Ross Hill Wines releases vintage series, signifying 10 years of carbon neutrality
summary:
The Orange region’s Ross Hill Wines has released a range of new vintage Pinnacle Series wines from winemaker Chris Jessop, marking 10 years of carbon neutral certification. The new wines comprise 2025 Ross Hill ‘Pinnacle Series’ Sauvignon Blanc ($35), 2025 Pinot Gris ($35), 2025 Chardonnay ($40), 2024 Shiraz ($50), 2024 Cabernet Franc ($50) and 2024 Cabernet Sauvignon ($50). Co-owner and vigneron James Robson described the winery’s carbon neutral positioning. “The initial achievement of gaining carbon neutral certification involved a great deal of innovation and effort, but now it is simply how we operate. We continue to innovate in the winery and vineyard, constantly challenging ourselves to lessen our footprint on the land.”

The post Orange’s Ross Hill Wines releases vintage series, signifying 10 years of carbon neutrality appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The winery has been carbon neutral certified for 10 years. Images supplied

The Orange region’s Ross Hill Wines has released a range of new vintage Pinnacle Series wines from winemaker Chris Jessop, marking 10 years of carbon neutral certification.

The new wines comprise 2025 Ross Hill ‘Pinnacle Series’ Sauvignon Blanc ($35), 2025 Pinot Gris ($35), 2025 Chardonnay ($40), 2024 Shiraz ($50), 2024 Cabernet Franc ($50) and 2024 Cabernet Sauvignon ($50).

Co-owner and vigneron James Robson described the winery’s carbon neutral positioning.

“The initial achievement of gaining carbon neutral certification involved a great deal of innovation and effort, but now it is simply how we operate.

“We continue to innovate in the winery and vineyard, constantly challenging ourselves to lessen our footprint on the land.”

Winemaker Jessop said the wines are a product of this philosophy.

“We are using less intervention in our winemaking practices.

“Throughout all the wines in the Pinnacle Series we are using a more supportive oak regime, allowing the pristine, Orange region fruit to step forward. Less new oak, a lighter toast, wild yeast fermentation and a gentler, considered hand has let ‘our place’ be the hero, with fruit purity and texture key drivers in the winemaking decisions,” he explained.

The majority of the grapes for the new Pinnacle Series wines come from Ross Hill’s 13-hectare Griffin Road Vineyard. Planted over 20 years ago, the winery said the vineyard is “well suited to these varieties”, with an elevation spanning 750 to 810 metres.

Jessop has been making wine at Ross Hill for the past two years, having previously made wine at Logan Wines (Mudgee), Grant Burge (Barossa), and Cape Jaffa (Limestone Coast). Jessop has also worked vintages in Germany including at Dr Loosen and, in his homeland, New Zealand.

“The elevation, aspect and soil of our Griffin Road vineyard reliably grow some of the region’s best grapes.

“When many Orange vineyards are marginal with their Cabernet and Cabernet Franc, Griffin Road physiologically ripens these varieties consistently. They are an absolute pleasure to bring into the winery,” said Jessop.

The Ross Hill Range is available nationally via Domaine Wine Shippers. Direct sales and the ‘1994’ Wine Club operate via their cellar door and through the Ross Hill Wines website.

title: ‘Mammoth’ Comedy in the Vines festival set to return for Yarra Valley
summary:
Comedy in the Vines will return to CherryHill Orchards in the Yarra Valley from 22 to 25 October, delivering “four huge days of world class comedy, premium wine experiences, live music, and unforgettable festival moments”. Created by Grapes of Mirth and supported by the Victorian Government’s Regional Events Fund as well as Tourism East, Comedy in the Vines is said to bring together “a raft of comedy stars, award-winning winemakers and unforgettable experiences in one spectacular destination”. The ‘Meet Your Maker Bar’ returns this year, featuring “more wineries, more wines and more opportunities to meet the people behind some of the Yarra Valley's finest drops”. Throughout the weekend, festivalgoers can enjoy tastings, masterclasses and cellar door experiences hosted by more than 20 of the region's best winemakers.

The post ‘Mammoth’ Comedy in the Vines festival set to return for Yarra Valley appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Festival director Rowan Smith said that this year’s festival is the biggest yet. Image supplied

Comedy in the Vines will return to CherryHill Orchards in the Yarra Valley from 22 to 25 October, delivering “four huge days of world class comedy, premium wine experiences, live music, and unforgettable festival moments”.

Created by Grapes of Mirth and supported by the Victorian Government’s Regional Events Fund as well as Tourism East, Comedy in the Vines is said to bring together “a raft of comedy stars, award-winning winemakers and unforgettable experiences in one spectacular destination”.

The ‘Meet Your Maker Bar’ returns this year, featuring “more wineries, more wines and more opportunities to meet the people behind some of the Yarra Valley’s finest drops”. Throughout the weekend, festivalgoers can enjoy tastings, masterclasses and cellar door experiences hosted by more than 20 of the region’s best winemakers.

Wine lovers “can sharpen their skills” at ‘Learn to Taste Wine Like a Pro’ with MW Meg Brodtmann, while those seeking a less conventional experience can join Nikki Britton for the festival’s “gloriously ridiculous” ‘Wine Aerobics’ session. The ‘5km Wine Run’ will also return on Sunday morning, inviting participants to “run, jog or stagger their way through the cherry orchards with wine tastings at every kilometre”.

Merrick Watts will once again lead the festivities as founder, host and ‘head clown’.

“Comedy in the Vines is the highlight of my year, where I get to bring together a bunch of my best mates in comedy and wine. This year I’ll be running a masterclass with one of the world’s best winemakers Sarah Crowe, teaching people how to taste wine with a certified Master of Wine, Meg Brodtmann, running the traditional Sunday morning heart starter 5k run with wine tastings along the way. Then onto the main stage, sitting down for a chat with Australian comedy royalty Santo Cilauro. Honestly, I can’t wait,” he said.

This year’s event will feature an Australian exclusive performance from UK comedian George Lewis, who has been dubbed “the funniest dad on Instagram” and become globally recognised because of his “relatable observations on family life, toddlers, parkrun enthusiasts and everyday absurdities”.

Comedy in the Vines festival director Rowan Smith highlighted the uniqueness of the festival.

Lewis is joined by more than 25 of Australia’s “most loved comedians” such as Lano & Woodley, Mel Buttle, Sammy J, Geraldine Hickey, Lloyd Langford, Lizzy Hoo, Dave Hughes, Claire Hooper, Tom Ballard, Chopper, Gillian Cosgriff, Dave Thornton, Sashi Perera, Ben Knight, Frankie McNair, Harry Jun, Nikki Britton, Dan Rath, Urvi Majumdar, Alex Ward, Kate Dolan and many more.

“Comedy in the Vines 2026 is the biggest festival we’ve ever put together [with] over 25 comedians, 20 winemakers, and four days of entertainment – it’s mammoth! We’re thrilled to be bringing George Lewis to Australia for the very first time. His online videos have been seen by nearly everyone, but after his huge sold-out UK stand-up tour, I know our audience is in for a treat. There really is nowhere else you can watch world class comedy, learn from a Master of Wine, run through cherry orchards drinking Pinot Noir, and end the night watching comedians battle it out as DJs. It’s a completely unique event – and this year is the best one yet,” he said

Presale tickets and more information can be found here.

title: ‘We make a wine for that’: National campaign set to return this August
summary:
This August, Wine Australia’s national campaign ‘We make a wine for that’ will return to “put Australian wine in front of consumers and bring it into more of today’s moments that matter”. Wine Australia said that the campaign’s 2025 launch “showed what’s possible when the sector comes together”, reaching 4 million people, delivering 6.4 million impressions across all digital channels, being viewed by over 2 million shoppers and driving more than 16,000 visits to the national events calendar. At its core, Wine Australia explained that the campaign is built around how people live and aims to connect wine to modern drinking occasions “from casual dinners to nights out and time with friends”. Andrew Shedden, general manager merchandise at Dan Murphy’s, said the strength of Australian wine lies in its diversity.

The post ‘We make a wine for that’: National campaign set to return this August appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The campaign aims to connect Australian wine to modern drinking occasions. Image supplied

This August, Wine Australia’s national campaign ‘We make a wine for that’ will return to “put Australian wine in front of consumers and bring it into more of today’s moments that matter”.

Wine Australia said that the campaign’s 2025 launch “showed what’s possible when the sector comes together”, reaching 4 million people, delivering 6.4 million impressions across all digital channels, being viewed by over 2 million shoppers and driving more than 16,000 visits to the national events calendar.

At its core, Wine Australia explained that the campaign is built around how people live and aims to connect wine to modern drinking occasions “from casual dinners to nights out and time with friends”.

Andrew Shedden, general manager merchandise at Dan Murphy’s, said the strength of Australian wine lies in its diversity.

“Younger consumers are exploring lighter styles, sparkling, rosé and categories like Prosecco and Spritz, and Australian winemakers are well placed to meet that demand. The diversity of what’s being produced domestically right now is outstanding, and campaigns like this are a great platform to showcase that breadth and quality,” Shedden said.

Wine Australia referenced retail data from Endeavour Group, which shows this preference shit across generations. For white wine, Chardonnay “continues to over-index” with older drinkers whilst Sauvignon Blanc remains strong with Gen X and Pinot Gris and Grigio gaining ground with younger Millennials and Gen Z.

Simultaneously, engagement is shifting towards rosé, sparkling and Prosecco, with Prosecco now “outperforming traditional sparkling” among Gen Z. Meanwhile, spritz-style drinks are reportedly “influencing how new consumers approach wine more broadly”.

As for red wine, younger consumers are entering the category through lighter styles like Pinot Noir rather than the historically favoured fuller-bodied wines.

Wine Australia said that the ‘We make a wine for that’ campaign allows the sector to meet consumers in the moments they are already choosing, enabling “businesses to showcase the breadth of Australian wines in ways that align to evolving tastes”.

Participation in the campaign is “straightforward” according to the organisation, with businesses able to submit events and experiences to the national campaign calendar to put activities directly in front of customers, “supported by paid digital, PR and social promotion throughout August”.

From cellar door tastings to food pairings, regional events and in-venue activations, the focus is on experiences that connect wine to real occasions, which Wine Australia says are the kinds of moments consumers are actively seeking out.

Participation in the campaign is open to the entire sector from individual cellar doors to regional initiatives.

Polly Mackarel, Sommelier at Infinity by Mark Best, said the campaign provides an effective way for venues to showcase Australian wine, in line with Infinity’s existing focus on local producers.

“It’s fantastic to support a platform that celebrates Australian wine and brings it to a broader audience across more occasions. At Infinity by Mark Best at Sydney Tower, we proudly feature only Australian producers, so this is a natural extension of our commitment to championing the quality of Australian wine.”

Campaign assets are now available, with businesses encouraged to start planning and submitting activations for August.

Event submission is available here.

Free campaign assets are available here.

The events calendar can be viewed here.

title: Moondah Brook brand and vineyard WA family owned once more
summary:
The Moondah Brook vineyard and wine brand has returned to Western Australian family ownership following its acquisition by the Bahen Family from Vinarchy. The agreement includes the historic Gingin vineyard, the Moondah Brook wine brand and its associated stock. The move returns Moondah Brook to local ownership and “creates an opportunity to build on the brand’s strong Western Australian heritage”. As part of the transaction, Vinarchy and the Bahen Family have entered into a long-term grape supply agreement, allowing fruit from the Moondah Brook vineyard to continue contributing to Vinarchy-owned Houghton wines. The Bahen Family said they are delighted to see the property return to local family ownership and are committed to ensuring it remains a productive and sustainable vineyard for the future. "Moondah Brook is a remarkable vineyard with a proud history and an important place in the Western Australian wine industry”.

The post Moondah Brook brand and vineyard WA family owned once more appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The brand, vineyard and associated stock have been handed over to the Bahen Family. Image supplied

The Moondah Brook vineyard and wine brand has returned to Western Australian family ownership following its acquisition by the Bahen Family from Vinarchy.

The agreement includes the historic Gingin vineyard, the Moondah Brook wine brand and its associated stock. The move returns Moondah Brook to local ownership and “creates an opportunity to build on the brand’s strong Western Australian heritage”.

As part of the transaction, Vinarchy and the Bahen Family have entered into a long-term grape supply agreement, allowing fruit from the Moondah Brook vineyard to continue contributing to Vinarchy-owned Houghton wines.

The Bahen Family said they are delighted to see the property return to local family ownership and are committed to ensuring it remains a productive and sustainable vineyard for the future.

“Moondah Brook is a remarkable vineyard with a proud history and an important place in the Western Australian wine industry.

“We also acknowledge the significant role Vinarchy and its predecessor organisations have played in developing and maintaining the vineyard over many years and are pleased to continue supporting the Houghton brand through the long-term grape supply agreement,” a spokesperson for the Bahen Family said.

Brett McKinnon, chief technical officer at Vinarchy, said the sale was a positive outcome for Moondah Brook and Houghton.

“Moondah Brook is a highly regarded vineyard with a long history in Western Australian wine, and Vinarchy has been proud to be part of that story.

“The vineyard has played a key role in Houghton wines over many years, so it was important to us to see that connection continued. We’re pleased to keep sourcing fruit from Moondah Brook, while seeing the brand enter its next chapter under new ownership,” he said.

Management of the vineyard will continue under the supervision of G5S Viticulture.

Calneggia Family Vineyards has been appointed as marketer and distributor of Moondah Brook wines as a part of the next chapter.

Jack Calneggia of Calneggia Family Vineyards said the opportunity to be involved in the revival of the Moondah Brook brand was both exciting and significant.

“Moondah Brook has a genuine Western Australian story and a strong foundation built on exceptional vineyard fruit.

“Our role is to honour that history while reintroducing the brand to consumers through wines that reflect both the character of the vineyard and the values of family ownership,” he said.

The Bahen Family emphasised that the transaction is a “positive outcome for all parties, providing certainty for vineyard operations, continuity of grape supply for Houghton wines and a pathway for the future development of the Moondah Brook brand”.

title: ASVO opens nominations for new regional directors
summary:
The Australian Society of Viticulture and Oenology (ASVO) invites applicants to apply for its four vacant regional director positions on its governing board. The organisation currently has openings for regional directors in Victoria, South Australia, New South Wales as well as a single position encompassing the states of Tasmania, Western Australia and Queensland. ASVO urges members who are based in the associated regions to explore the opportunity with applications due Friday 28 August. “Having regional directors from different states means that there is a champion for each state on the board...we are grateful for the dedicated contribution of the incumbent regional directors over recent years,” said Dr Samantha Scarratt, ASVO president.

The post ASVO opens nominations for new regional directors appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The Australian Society of Viticulture and Oenology (ASVO) invites applicants to apply for its four vacant regional director positions on its governing board.

The organisation currently has openings for regional directors in Victoria, South Australia, New South Wales as well as a single position encompassing the states of Tasmania, Western Australia and Queensland.

ASVO urges members who are based in the associated regions to explore the opportunity with applications due Friday 28 August.

“Having regional directors from different states means that there is a champion for each state on the board…we are grateful for the dedicated contribution of the incumbent regional directors over recent years,” said Dr Samantha Scarratt, ASVO president.

As a national professional membership body for individuals in the wine and grape sector, ASVO said that the appointment of regional directors “ensures widespread geographical representation on the ASVO board”.

The position requires directors to serve for a three-year term, with appointees to be officially announced during the annual general meeting in November.

President Scarratt shared that regional directors will play a crucial role in “planning potential activities and strategic direction for the ASVO.”

These vacancies have arisen due to the upcoming expiry of the terms of ASVO regional directors Andy Clarke (VIC), who is also the current Vice President; Jeremy Dineen (TAS, WA and QLD); Chrissie Smith (NSW); and Dr Alana Seabrook (SA) later this year.

Additional details are available here.

title: Wine tasting becomes a spectator sport in a competition worth backing
summary:
Wine-media brand Bottle Shock has opened the 2026 season of its ‘Big Blind League’, a year-long competitive blind-tasting competition run as a sport. The competition is open to any South Australian resident aged 18 and over from trade professionals to total beginners. Better yet – there’s no initial sign-up fee. Bottle Shock described the format as “simple and a little ruthless” with wines being poured into black glasses for players to taste. Bottle Shock founder Brendan Carter explained to Daily Wine News how participants can rack up points. “Anyone who comes to Bottle Shock in Gumeracha and purchases flights ($25) immediately receives five points on the board. If they complete the tasks on those flights, depending on the mission, they can earn an additional 10 points by, for example, ordering items from cheapest to most expensive or identifying the odd one out.” Carter is hoping for the support of venue owners and sponsors to help the project hit the ground running.

The post Wine tasting becomes a spectator sport in a competition worth backing appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The competition aims to help wine channel the same energy as a spectator sport. Image supplied

By Kyra Trussell

Wine-media brand Bottle Shock has opened the 2026 season of its ‘Big Blind League’, a year-long competitive blind-tasting competition run as a sport.

The competition is open to any South Australian resident aged 18 and over from trade professionals to total beginners. Better yet – there’s no initial sign-up fee.

Bottle Shock described the format as “simple and a little ruthless” with wines being poured into black glasses for players to taste.

Bottle Shock founder Brendan Carter explained to Daily Wine News how participants can rack up points.

“Anyone who comes to Bottle Shock in Gumeracha and purchases flights ($25) immediately receives five points on the board. If they complete the tasks on those flights, depending on the mission, they can earn an additional 10 points by, for example, ordering items from cheapest to most expensive or identifying the odd one out.

“At regional cups, points are awarded for participation, which is quite significant. We view the regional cups as major central events where participants can greatly increase their points and ladder standings; they are essentially points super‑events for competitors.

“Those who rank in the regional cups earn points, and the first‑place finisher receives a ‘free run’ into the grand final at the end of the year, eliminating the need to compete further. The total number of points a competitor has determines their final seeding in the grand final,” Carter said.

Providing a few personal details to your palette profile after signing up is a free way to earn five points.

Moreover, regional cups cost $25 to enter and earn participants 15 points each just for showing up. If a participant makes it to the grand final, there are no further costs.

Points climb a live public ladder with the top 24 tasters facing off at a grand final on Saturday 5 December in Gumeracha, where the wines come from anywhere around the world and the last palate standing takes home $5,000.

The League grew from a one-off experiment in 2025 with the same premise called the ‘5k Big Blind’. The 2025 event drew more than 160 entrants over four months, narrowed to a final 24 and ended with a full-day of tasting. The winner was a man named Stefan, a bacteriologist with no wine-industry background, who beat a room of seasoned professionals. Not one taster achieved a perfect score that day.

“Our aim is to show the average punter what wine can do: a smell or taste can transport you to another place. Seeing young people do this on screen helps other young people feel less intimidated when they first approach wine. It isn’t about health claims or pressure to buy; it’s pure curiosity – how can a sip or a sniff take you somewhere else? That’s something wine does better than anything else you could put in your mouth,” Carter shared.

When asked how he came up with the idea, Carter referenced “the late great Australian” Len Evans who created the ‘Options Game’ – a tasting format now used worldwide to teach the flavours of a region – and the historical ‘Judgement of Paris’ blind tasting.

“We wanted to add to that heritage by turning wine tasting into a spectator sport. We’ve combined elements of the ‘Judgment of Paris’ and the ‘Options Game’, first on our YouTube channel and then at the Bottle Shock Bar, where viewers become competitors. Now we’re ready to take the experience on the road, showing that blind‑tasting wine can be both a spectator and a competitive sport.”

Dates are locked in for the regional cups, although Carter said that the venues are still being finalised.

11 July – Clare Cup

22 August – Adelaide Hills Cup

19 September – Barossa Bowl

24 October – McLaren Vale Cup

14 November – Coonawarra Cup

5 December – Grand Final (Bottle Shock, Gumeracha)

Carter is hoping for the support of venue owners and sponsors to help the project hit the ground running.

“All the biggest breakthroughs in the Australian wine industry have come from collaboration rather than competition, so we’re trying something that’s never been done before. We’ve found a way to educate people through entertainment – by turning learning into a sport. Instead of focusing on consumption, we’re gamifying moderation and showcasing the 85% of the wine experience that rarely gets talked about. The remaining 15% is the alcohol, but that’s not what excites us.

“Wine is really about provenance, place and culture. By gamifying the 85%, we hope venues, sponsors and supporters will help us launch this project. It’s a costly, complex endeavour, but it could bring much‑needed hope and joy to an industry that’s increasingly lacking it,” he emphasised.

For individuals looking to support the competition, Carter said that heading to the website and creating a profile is the easiest way.

“Start by visiting the Bottle Shock Bar in Gumeracha, buying a flight and testing your palate. Then head out into our regions and experience how wine reflects the land – the sight, soil, sunshine and the amazing people behind it.

“That’s how you support the competition: share it with your mates. Everyone knows a friend who thinks they’re a bit of a wine person. Invite them to give it a go and cheer them on. It’s not just about the competition; it’s about having a good time watching your mates win or lose – a classic Australian pastime.”

As for what attendees can expect at the cups, Carter flagged “amazing hospitality, plenty of fun, plenty of laughs, amazing wine and fun challenges”.

“At the core of wine lies hospitality and culture. If it isn’t fun and approachable, we’ve missed the very value wine brings to people’s lives.”

More information and sign-ups can be found here.

title: Friday Feedback 03/07
summary:
If you’ve been reading the news and wondering what your peers think of it, look no further. Our Friday Feedback segment provides a platform for the voices of our readers, highlighting the thoughts, reflections and musings of members of the wine industry. content:
If you’ve been reading the news and wondering what your peers think of it, look no further.

Our Friday Feedback segment provides a platform for the voices of our readers, highlighting the thoughts, reflections and musings of members of the wine industry.

 

Read our recent roundup here:

 

Responding to New Wine Australia investment agreement secures AWRI research and industry services to 2030

“The AWRI has been exemplary in the manner it has conducted itself for the entire time of my winemaking journey on the Granite Belt – 42yrs. The high level of research and implementation is to be praised. The staffing has been excellent. This is a body which has over and over demonstrated innovation and support for the wine industry. The devising of the GG Assay and the Closure trials are two examples that spring to mind. The MRL data base has been a pivotal resource base.

Well done.”

  • Tony Comino

 

Responding to Grillo

“Thanks for all the great info. we just bottled out first Grillo and it’s interesting to get others’ experience.”

  • Jefferson A Hinchliffe

title: Central Otago’s Young Winemaker and Young Viticulturist of the Year winners announced
summary:
The winners for Central Otago’s Young Winemaker and Young Viticulturist competition were announced yesterday at a combined awards dinner held at Bannockburn Hall. Lisa Fromont from Te Kano won the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey Central Otago Young Winemaker of the Year award whilst Matthew Kerruish from Vinewise Viticulture took home the 2026 Ormond Nurseries Central Otago Young Viticulturist of the Year title. Fromont is primarily based at Te Kano’s Waitaki Estate, where she is 2IC in both the vineyards and the winery. Kerruish works for Vinewise as well as at his family vineyards and winery Folding Hills Wines in Bendigo. At the same award night, Joshua Irving from Lincoln University became the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey North Canterbury Young Winemaker of the Year.

The post Central Otago’s Young Winemaker and Young Viticulturist of the Year winners announced appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The Viticulturist of the Year contestants. Images supplied

The winners for Central Otago’s Young Winemaker and Young Viticulturist competition were announced yesterday at a combined awards dinner held at Bannockburn Hall.

Lisa Fromont from Te Kano won the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey Central Otago Young Winemaker of the Year award whilst Matthew Kerruish from Vinewise Viticulture took home the 2026 Ormond Nurseries Central Otago Young Viticulturist of the Year title.

Fromont is primarily based at Te Kano’s Waitaki Estate, where she is 2IC in both the vineyards and the winery. Kerruish works for Vinewise as well as at his family vineyards and winery Folding Hills Wines in Bendigo.

At the same award night, Joshua Irving from Lincoln University became the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey North Canterbury Young Winemaker of the Year.

Irving also recently became this year’s North Canterbury Young Viticulturist of the Year. Due to winning the title for each competition, he will be the first person ever to compete in both the Young Viticulturist and Young Winemaker national finals in the same year.

“I know there’s a lot of hard work ahead, but I see it as a great opportunity and am excited to seize it,” he said.

Young Winemaker contestants

Congratulations also go to Matt O’Connor from Prophet’s Rock who came third in the Young Winemaker competition.

Rachel Tonk won the Constellation Brands Laboratory Section and Fromont took out the Fruitfed Supplies Best Speech and the Indevin Wine Judging section.

The other contestants were Lachie John, Mount Edward; Maggie Wise, VinPro; Matthew Kerruish, Matt Connell Wines; and Regina Marks, Monte Christo.

Young Viticulturist contestants

The Young Viticulturist competition was held at Otago Polytechnic’s Central Campus on Thursday.

New Zealand Winegrowers congratulated runner ups Braxton Benseman from Mt Difficulty who came second and to Chavi Cleworth from Carrick who came third.

Cleworth also won the BioStart Hortisports whilst Benseman came first in the Syngenta Biologicals Nutrition Section. Jack McMonagle took out the head-to-head Ecotrellis section.

Savannah McGhie from Burn Cottage was awarded the Ormond Nurseries Professional Reputation Award for her “positive, friendly attitude as well as huge growth over the last year”.

The other contestants included Olive Roberts (Peregrine), Abby Crawford and Hamish Anderson – both from Felton Road.

The National Finals

Matthew Kerruish is reportedly thrilled to go through to the national final and is “determined to do Central Otago proud” as he competes for the overall title of 2026 Ormond Nurseries Young Viticulturist of the Year, being held in August.

Lisa Fromont will be on the Burgundy Exchange, however, that experience has a date clash with the national finals, also being held in August. This means she will not be representing Central Otago at the national finals of the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey Young Winemaker of the Year.

The regional chairs were Annabel Bulk and Larissa Woods from Felton Road. Having both competed previously, New Zealand Winegrowers said the two “enjoy giving back and helping the next generation come through”.

title: ‘Beyond the Bottle’ trade event set for Melbourne
summary:
Alepat Taylor will host a dedicated wine and hospitality trade day in Melbourne at Rydges on 27 July. The event, titled ‘Beyond the Bottle 2026, is designed to “educate, inspire and connect the industry through world-class producers, premium food and cutting-edge hospitality solutions”. Open exclusively to trade and free to attend, the event will bring together over 35 international and domestic producers. Organisers claim the event presents a “rare opportunity for sommeliers, buyers and operators to engage with the people behind the wines and deepen their understanding of regional styles, philosophy and production”.

The post ‘Beyond the Bottle’ trade event set for Melbourne appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The event aims to bring together sommeliers, buyers and operators from around the world. Image supplied

Alepat Taylor will host a dedicated wine and hospitality trade day in Melbourne at Rydges on 27 July.

The event, titled ‘Beyond the Bottle 2026, is designed to “educate, inspire and connect the industry through world-class producers, premium food and cutting-edge hospitality solutions”.

Open exclusively to trade and free to attend, the event will bring together over 35 international and domestic producers. Organisers claim the event presents a “rare opportunity for sommeliers, buyers and operators to engage with the people behind the wines and deepen their understanding of regional styles, philosophy and production”.

Alepat Taylor said that a key focus for this year’s event is “education through exploration”, with a “standout” Italian offering that it believes showcases the country in its entirety and therefore helps attendees build “a comprehensive understanding of one of the world’s most diverse wine landscapes”.

The event will also highlight Australia’s own wine landscape with a selection from South Australia’s Barossa Valley and Clare Valley through to Victoria’s Yarra Valley.

Bruno 1956 (formerly Bruno Fine Foods) will present a range of premium Italian gourmet products while Cedar Hospitality displays a range of catering equipment and service solutions.

The event will host three “exclusive VIP masterclasses, providing focused deep dives into key themes and categories”:

  1. Cordero di Montezemolo |

Barolo vertical tasting with Alberto Cordero di Montezemolo

  1. New Custodians of the Barossa

Discussion on the future of the Barossa with Sophie Melton of Charles Melton Wines, Adam Fiegert of Gibson and Damien Tscharke of Tscharke Wines

  1. L’Art de l’Expression

Maxence Goinere from Camus Cognac will delve into the terroir of Cognac and the blending experience

These sessions are designed for trade professionals “seeking deeper, actionable knowledge, with insights delivered directly by producers and industry experts”.

title: California Wines partners with Sydney steakhouse for July-long wine showcase
summary:
California Wines has partnered with Sydney steakhouse The Gidley to present a month-long showcase “celebrating the diversity and quality” of California’s wine regions. The showcase has been designed to introduce Sydney wine lovers to the breadth of styles, appellations and varietals produced across the Golden State. California Wines’ joint regional director for North Asia and Australasia Hiro Tejima said that Australian wine drinkers may only know California for Cabernet Sauvignon expressions – however, he believes that’s only a fraction of what it has to offer. "Australian wine drinkers may know California for certain expressions of Cabernet Sauvignon, but that's only a fraction of what the Golden State has to offer. California is one of the most dynamic wine producing regions in the world, with everything from cool-climate Chardonnay and Pinot Noir to age-worthy reds including Cinsault and Zinfandel from historic old-vine vineyards.”

The post California Wines partners with Sydney steakhouse for July-long wine showcase appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The wines have been curated by Emma Chappell, head sommelier at The Gidley’s. Image credit Dominic Loneragan

California Wines has partnered with Sydney steakhouse The Gidley to present a month-long showcase “celebrating the diversity and quality” of California’s wine regions.

The showcase has been designed to introduce Sydney wine lovers to the breadth of styles, appellations and varietals produced across the Golden State.

California Wines’ joint regional director for North Asia and Australasia Hiro Tejima said that Australian wine drinkers may only know California for Cabernet Sauvignon expressions – however, he believes that’s only a fraction of what it has to offer.

“Australian wine drinkers may know California for certain expressions of Cabernet Sauvignon, but that’s only a fraction of what the Golden State has to offer. California is one of the most dynamic wine producing regions in the world, with everything from cool-climate Chardonnay and Pinot Noir to age-worthy reds including Cinsault and Zinfandel from historic old-vine vineyards.

“As Americans prepare to celebrate Independence Day and the 250th anniversary of the United States, this partnership with The Gidley provides a fitting opportunity for Sydneysiders to discover a wider range of Californian wine through a carefully curated selection of producers and regions,” he said.

Launching on 3 July, the showcase has been curated by The Gidley’s head sommelier Emma Chappel and features 31 Californian wines available by the bottle, with seven new wines not already on The Gidley menu being exclusively available for the duration of the showcase.

When curating the list, Chappell said she wanted to showcase both the wines people expect from California and some they may have never encountered before.

Across the restaurant and lounge, the venue will also offer a California by-the-glass program featuring seven wines, “allowing guests to explore a range of regions and styles without committing to a full bottle”.

“Of course, there are iconic Cabernet Sauvs and Chardonnays, but there are also varieties like Trousseau and Syrah, even sparkling, that demonstrate just how diverse and exciting California has become. The by-the-glass program allows guests to explore multiple regions and styles in a single visit, which is something very few wine lists can offer,” she shared.

The wine menu is subject to demand and will “evolve throughout the month” with rotating selections.

Best known for regions like the Napa Valley and Sonoma County, California produces more than 80% of all American wine and is home to “a diverse collection of coastal, mountain and inland wine regions”.

The restaurant will also offer an “exclusive” Pastrami Beef Rib with Cabernet Glaze and Pickled Cucumber for the duration of the wine promotion.

title: Orange’s Ross Hill Wines releases vintage series, signifying 10 years of carbon neutrality
summary:
The Orange region’s Ross Hill Wines has released a range of new vintage Pinnacle Series wines from winemaker Chris Jessop, marking 10 years of carbon neutral certification. The new wines comprise 2025 Ross Hill ‘Pinnacle Series’ Sauvignon Blanc ($35), 2025 Pinot Gris ($35), 2025 Chardonnay ($40), 2024 Shiraz ($50), 2024 Cabernet Franc ($50) and 2024 Cabernet Sauvignon ($50). Co-owner and vigneron James Robson described the winery’s carbon neutral positioning. “The initial achievement of gaining carbon neutral certification involved a great deal of innovation and effort, but now it is simply how we operate. We continue to innovate in the winery and vineyard, constantly challenging ourselves to lessen our footprint on the land.”

The post Orange’s Ross Hill Wines releases vintage series, signifying 10 years of carbon neutrality appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The winery has been carbon neutral certified for 10 years. Images supplied

The Orange region’s Ross Hill Wines has released a range of new vintage Pinnacle Series wines from winemaker Chris Jessop, marking 10 years of carbon neutral certification.

The new wines comprise 2025 Ross Hill ‘Pinnacle Series’ Sauvignon Blanc ($35), 2025 Pinot Gris ($35), 2025 Chardonnay ($40), 2024 Shiraz ($50), 2024 Cabernet Franc ($50) and 2024 Cabernet Sauvignon ($50).

Co-owner and vigneron James Robson described the winery’s carbon neutral positioning.

“The initial achievement of gaining carbon neutral certification involved a great deal of innovation and effort, but now it is simply how we operate.

“We continue to innovate in the winery and vineyard, constantly challenging ourselves to lessen our footprint on the land.”

Winemaker Jessop said the wines are a product of this philosophy.

“We are using less intervention in our winemaking practices.

“Throughout all the wines in the Pinnacle Series we are using a more supportive oak regime, allowing the pristine, Orange region fruit to step forward. Less new oak, a lighter toast, wild yeast fermentation and a gentler, considered hand has let ‘our place’ be the hero, with fruit purity and texture key drivers in the winemaking decisions,” he explained.

The majority of the grapes for the new Pinnacle Series wines come from Ross Hill’s 13-hectare Griffin Road Vineyard. Planted over 20 years ago, the winery said the vineyard is “well suited to these varieties”, with an elevation spanning 750 to 810 metres.

Jessop has been making wine at Ross Hill for the past two years, having previously made wine at Logan Wines (Mudgee), Grant Burge (Barossa), and Cape Jaffa (Limestone Coast). Jessop has also worked vintages in Germany including at Dr Loosen and, in his homeland, New Zealand.

“The elevation, aspect and soil of our Griffin Road vineyard reliably grow some of the region’s best grapes.

“When many Orange vineyards are marginal with their Cabernet and Cabernet Franc, Griffin Road physiologically ripens these varieties consistently. They are an absolute pleasure to bring into the winery,” said Jessop.

The Ross Hill Range is available nationally via Domaine Wine Shippers. Direct sales and the ‘1994’ Wine Club operate via their cellar door and through the Ross Hill Wines website.

title: ‘Mammoth’ Comedy in the Vines festival set to return for Yarra Valley
summary:
Comedy in the Vines will return to CherryHill Orchards in the Yarra Valley from 22 to 25 October, delivering “four huge days of world class comedy, premium wine experiences, live music, and unforgettable festival moments”. Created by Grapes of Mirth and supported by the Victorian Government’s Regional Events Fund as well as Tourism East, Comedy in the Vines is said to bring together “a raft of comedy stars, award-winning winemakers and unforgettable experiences in one spectacular destination”. The ‘Meet Your Maker Bar’ returns this year, featuring “more wineries, more wines and more opportunities to meet the people behind some of the Yarra Valley's finest drops”. Throughout the weekend, festivalgoers can enjoy tastings, masterclasses and cellar door experiences hosted by more than 20 of the region's best winemakers.

The post ‘Mammoth’ Comedy in the Vines festival set to return for Yarra Valley appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Festival director Rowan Smith said that this year’s festival is the biggest yet. Image supplied

Comedy in the Vines will return to CherryHill Orchards in the Yarra Valley from 22 to 25 October, delivering “four huge days of world class comedy, premium wine experiences, live music, and unforgettable festival moments”.

Created by Grapes of Mirth and supported by the Victorian Government’s Regional Events Fund as well as Tourism East, Comedy in the Vines is said to bring together “a raft of comedy stars, award-winning winemakers and unforgettable experiences in one spectacular destination”.

The ‘Meet Your Maker Bar’ returns this year, featuring “more wineries, more wines and more opportunities to meet the people behind some of the Yarra Valley’s finest drops”. Throughout the weekend, festivalgoers can enjoy tastings, masterclasses and cellar door experiences hosted by more than 20 of the region’s best winemakers.

Wine lovers “can sharpen their skills” at ‘Learn to Taste Wine Like a Pro’ with MW Meg Brodtmann, while those seeking a less conventional experience can join Nikki Britton for the festival’s “gloriously ridiculous” ‘Wine Aerobics’ session. The ‘5km Wine Run’ will also return on Sunday morning, inviting participants to “run, jog or stagger their way through the cherry orchards with wine tastings at every kilometre”.

Merrick Watts will once again lead the festivities as founder, host and ‘head clown’.

“Comedy in the Vines is the highlight of my year, where I get to bring together a bunch of my best mates in comedy and wine. This year I’ll be running a masterclass with one of the world’s best winemakers Sarah Crowe, teaching people how to taste wine with a certified Master of Wine, Meg Brodtmann, running the traditional Sunday morning heart starter 5k run with wine tastings along the way. Then onto the main stage, sitting down for a chat with Australian comedy royalty Santo Cilauro. Honestly, I can’t wait,” he said.

This year’s event will feature an Australian exclusive performance from UK comedian George Lewis, who has been dubbed “the funniest dad on Instagram” and become globally recognised because of his “relatable observations on family life, toddlers, parkrun enthusiasts and everyday absurdities”.

Comedy in the Vines festival director Rowan Smith highlighted the uniqueness of the festival.

Lewis is joined by more than 25 of Australia’s “most loved comedians” such as Lano & Woodley, Mel Buttle, Sammy J, Geraldine Hickey, Lloyd Langford, Lizzy Hoo, Dave Hughes, Claire Hooper, Tom Ballard, Chopper, Gillian Cosgriff, Dave Thornton, Sashi Perera, Ben Knight, Frankie McNair, Harry Jun, Nikki Britton, Dan Rath, Urvi Majumdar, Alex Ward, Kate Dolan and many more.

“Comedy in the Vines 2026 is the biggest festival we’ve ever put together [with] over 25 comedians, 20 winemakers, and four days of entertainment – it’s mammoth! We’re thrilled to be bringing George Lewis to Australia for the very first time. His online videos have been seen by nearly everyone, but after his huge sold-out UK stand-up tour, I know our audience is in for a treat. There really is nowhere else you can watch world class comedy, learn from a Master of Wine, run through cherry orchards drinking Pinot Noir, and end the night watching comedians battle it out as DJs. It’s a completely unique event – and this year is the best one yet,” he said

Presale tickets and more information can be found here.

title: ‘We make a wine for that’: National campaign set to return this August
summary:
This August, Wine Australia’s national campaign ‘We make a wine for that’ will return to “put Australian wine in front of consumers and bring it into more of today’s moments that matter”. Wine Australia said that the campaign’s 2025 launch “showed what’s possible when the sector comes together”, reaching 4 million people, delivering 6.4 million impressions across all digital channels, being viewed by over 2 million shoppers and driving more than 16,000 visits to the national events calendar. At its core, Wine Australia explained that the campaign is built around how people live and aims to connect wine to modern drinking occasions “from casual dinners to nights out and time with friends”. Andrew Shedden, general manager merchandise at Dan Murphy’s, said the strength of Australian wine lies in its diversity.

The post ‘We make a wine for that’: National campaign set to return this August appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The campaign aims to connect Australian wine to modern drinking occasions. Image supplied

This August, Wine Australia’s national campaign ‘We make a wine for that’ will return to “put Australian wine in front of consumers and bring it into more of today’s moments that matter”.

Wine Australia said that the campaign’s 2025 launch “showed what’s possible when the sector comes together”, reaching 4 million people, delivering 6.4 million impressions across all digital channels, being viewed by over 2 million shoppers and driving more than 16,000 visits to the national events calendar.

At its core, Wine Australia explained that the campaign is built around how people live and aims to connect wine to modern drinking occasions “from casual dinners to nights out and time with friends”.

Andrew Shedden, general manager merchandise at Dan Murphy’s, said the strength of Australian wine lies in its diversity.

“Younger consumers are exploring lighter styles, sparkling, rosé and categories like Prosecco and Spritz, and Australian winemakers are well placed to meet that demand. The diversity of what’s being produced domestically right now is outstanding, and campaigns like this are a great platform to showcase that breadth and quality,” Shedden said.

Wine Australia referenced retail data from Endeavour Group, which shows this preference shit across generations. For white wine, Chardonnay “continues to over-index” with older drinkers whilst Sauvignon Blanc remains strong with Gen X and Pinot Gris and Grigio gaining ground with younger Millennials and Gen Z.

Simultaneously, engagement is shifting towards rosé, sparkling and Prosecco, with Prosecco now “outperforming traditional sparkling” among Gen Z. Meanwhile, spritz-style drinks are reportedly “influencing how new consumers approach wine more broadly”.

As for red wine, younger consumers are entering the category through lighter styles like Pinot Noir rather than the historically favoured fuller-bodied wines.

Wine Australia said that the ‘We make a wine for that’ campaign allows the sector to meet consumers in the moments they are already choosing, enabling “businesses to showcase the breadth of Australian wines in ways that align to evolving tastes”.

Participation in the campaign is “straightforward” according to the organisation, with businesses able to submit events and experiences to the national campaign calendar to put activities directly in front of customers, “supported by paid digital, PR and social promotion throughout August”.

From cellar door tastings to food pairings, regional events and in-venue activations, the focus is on experiences that connect wine to real occasions, which Wine Australia says are the kinds of moments consumers are actively seeking out.

Participation in the campaign is open to the entire sector from individual cellar doors to regional initiatives.

Polly Mackarel, Sommelier at Infinity by Mark Best, said the campaign provides an effective way for venues to showcase Australian wine, in line with Infinity’s existing focus on local producers.

“It’s fantastic to support a platform that celebrates Australian wine and brings it to a broader audience across more occasions. At Infinity by Mark Best at Sydney Tower, we proudly feature only Australian producers, so this is a natural extension of our commitment to championing the quality of Australian wine.”

Campaign assets are now available, with businesses encouraged to start planning and submitting activations for August.

Event submission is available here.

Free campaign assets are available here.

The events calendar can be viewed here.

title: Moondah Brook brand and vineyard WA family owned once more
summary:
The Moondah Brook vineyard and wine brand has returned to Western Australian family ownership following its acquisition by the Bahen Family from Vinarchy. The agreement includes the historic Gingin vineyard, the Moondah Brook wine brand and its associated stock. The move returns Moondah Brook to local ownership and “creates an opportunity to build on the brand’s strong Western Australian heritage”. As part of the transaction, Vinarchy and the Bahen Family have entered into a long-term grape supply agreement, allowing fruit from the Moondah Brook vineyard to continue contributing to Vinarchy-owned Houghton wines. The Bahen Family said they are delighted to see the property return to local family ownership and are committed to ensuring it remains a productive and sustainable vineyard for the future. "Moondah Brook is a remarkable vineyard with a proud history and an important place in the Western Australian wine industry”.

The post Moondah Brook brand and vineyard WA family owned once more appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The brand, vineyard and associated stock have been handed over to the Bahen Family. Image supplied

The Moondah Brook vineyard and wine brand has returned to Western Australian family ownership following its acquisition by the Bahen Family from Vinarchy.

The agreement includes the historic Gingin vineyard, the Moondah Brook wine brand and its associated stock. The move returns Moondah Brook to local ownership and “creates an opportunity to build on the brand’s strong Western Australian heritage”.

As part of the transaction, Vinarchy and the Bahen Family have entered into a long-term grape supply agreement, allowing fruit from the Moondah Brook vineyard to continue contributing to Vinarchy-owned Houghton wines.

The Bahen Family said they are delighted to see the property return to local family ownership and are committed to ensuring it remains a productive and sustainable vineyard for the future.

“Moondah Brook is a remarkable vineyard with a proud history and an important place in the Western Australian wine industry.

“We also acknowledge the significant role Vinarchy and its predecessor organisations have played in developing and maintaining the vineyard over many years and are pleased to continue supporting the Houghton brand through the long-term grape supply agreement,” a spokesperson for the Bahen Family said.

Brett McKinnon, chief technical officer at Vinarchy, said the sale was a positive outcome for Moondah Brook and Houghton.

“Moondah Brook is a highly regarded vineyard with a long history in Western Australian wine, and Vinarchy has been proud to be part of that story.

“The vineyard has played a key role in Houghton wines over many years, so it was important to us to see that connection continued. We’re pleased to keep sourcing fruit from Moondah Brook, while seeing the brand enter its next chapter under new ownership,” he said.

Management of the vineyard will continue under the supervision of G5S Viticulture.

Calneggia Family Vineyards has been appointed as marketer and distributor of Moondah Brook wines as a part of the next chapter.

Jack Calneggia of Calneggia Family Vineyards said the opportunity to be involved in the revival of the Moondah Brook brand was both exciting and significant.

“Moondah Brook has a genuine Western Australian story and a strong foundation built on exceptional vineyard fruit.

“Our role is to honour that history while reintroducing the brand to consumers through wines that reflect both the character of the vineyard and the values of family ownership,” he said.

The Bahen Family emphasised that the transaction is a “positive outcome for all parties, providing certainty for vineyard operations, continuity of grape supply for Houghton wines and a pathway for the future development of the Moondah Brook brand”.

title: ASVO opens nominations for new regional directors
summary:
The Australian Society of Viticulture and Oenology (ASVO) invites applicants to apply for its four vacant regional director positions on its governing board. The organisation currently has openings for regional directors in Victoria, South Australia, New South Wales as well as a single position encompassing the states of Tasmania, Western Australia and Queensland. ASVO urges members who are based in the associated regions to explore the opportunity with applications due Friday 28 August. “Having regional directors from different states means that there is a champion for each state on the board...we are grateful for the dedicated contribution of the incumbent regional directors over recent years,” said Dr Samantha Scarratt, ASVO president.

The post ASVO opens nominations for new regional directors appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The Australian Society of Viticulture and Oenology (ASVO) invites applicants to apply for its four vacant regional director positions on its governing board.

The organisation currently has openings for regional directors in Victoria, South Australia, New South Wales as well as a single position encompassing the states of Tasmania, Western Australia and Queensland.

ASVO urges members who are based in the associated regions to explore the opportunity with applications due Friday 28 August.

“Having regional directors from different states means that there is a champion for each state on the board…we are grateful for the dedicated contribution of the incumbent regional directors over recent years,” said Dr Samantha Scarratt, ASVO president.

As a national professional membership body for individuals in the wine and grape sector, ASVO said that the appointment of regional directors “ensures widespread geographical representation on the ASVO board”.

The position requires directors to serve for a three-year term, with appointees to be officially announced during the annual general meeting in November.

President Scarratt shared that regional directors will play a crucial role in “planning potential activities and strategic direction for the ASVO.”

These vacancies have arisen due to the upcoming expiry of the terms of ASVO regional directors Andy Clarke (VIC), who is also the current Vice President; Jeremy Dineen (TAS, WA and QLD); Chrissie Smith (NSW); and Dr Alana Seabrook (SA) later this year.

Additional details are available here.

title: Wine tasting becomes a spectator sport in a competition worth backing
summary:
Wine-media brand Bottle Shock has opened the 2026 season of its ‘Big Blind League’, a year-long competitive blind-tasting competition run as a sport. The competition is open to any South Australian resident aged 18 and over from trade professionals to total beginners. Better yet – there’s no initial sign-up fee. Bottle Shock described the format as “simple and a little ruthless” with wines being poured into black glasses for players to taste. Bottle Shock founder Brendan Carter explained to Daily Wine News how participants can rack up points. “Anyone who comes to Bottle Shock in Gumeracha and purchases flights ($25) immediately receives five points on the board. If they complete the tasks on those flights, depending on the mission, they can earn an additional 10 points by, for example, ordering items from cheapest to most expensive or identifying the odd one out.” Carter is hoping for the support of venue owners and sponsors to help the project hit the ground running.

The post Wine tasting becomes a spectator sport in a competition worth backing appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The competition aims to help wine channel the same energy as a spectator sport. Image supplied

By Kyra Trussell

Wine-media brand Bottle Shock has opened the 2026 season of its ‘Big Blind League’, a year-long competitive blind-tasting competition run as a sport.

The competition is open to any South Australian resident aged 18 and over from trade professionals to total beginners. Better yet – there’s no initial sign-up fee.

Bottle Shock described the format as “simple and a little ruthless” with wines being poured into black glasses for players to taste.

Bottle Shock founder Brendan Carter explained to Daily Wine News how participants can rack up points.

“Anyone who comes to Bottle Shock in Gumeracha and purchases flights ($25) immediately receives five points on the board. If they complete the tasks on those flights, depending on the mission, they can earn an additional 10 points by, for example, ordering items from cheapest to most expensive or identifying the odd one out.

“At regional cups, points are awarded for participation, which is quite significant. We view the regional cups as major central events where participants can greatly increase their points and ladder standings; they are essentially points super‑events for competitors.

“Those who rank in the regional cups earn points, and the first‑place finisher receives a ‘free run’ into the grand final at the end of the year, eliminating the need to compete further. The total number of points a competitor has determines their final seeding in the grand final,” Carter said.

Providing a few personal details to your palette profile after signing up is a free way to earn five points.

Moreover, regional cups cost $25 to enter and earn participants 15 points each just for showing up. If a participant makes it to the grand final, there are no further costs.

Points climb a live public ladder with the top 24 tasters facing off at a grand final on Saturday 5 December in Gumeracha, where the wines come from anywhere around the world and the last palate standing takes home $5,000.

The League grew from a one-off experiment in 2025 with the same premise called the ‘5k Big Blind’. The 2025 event drew more than 160 entrants over four months, narrowed to a final 24 and ended with a full-day of tasting. The winner was a man named Stefan, a bacteriologist with no wine-industry background, who beat a room of seasoned professionals. Not one taster achieved a perfect score that day.

“Our aim is to show the average punter what wine can do: a smell or taste can transport you to another place. Seeing young people do this on screen helps other young people feel less intimidated when they first approach wine. It isn’t about health claims or pressure to buy; it’s pure curiosity – how can a sip or a sniff take you somewhere else? That’s something wine does better than anything else you could put in your mouth,” Carter shared.

When asked how he came up with the idea, Carter referenced “the late great Australian” Len Evans who created the ‘Options Game’ – a tasting format now used worldwide to teach the flavours of a region – and the historical ‘Judgement of Paris’ blind tasting.

“We wanted to add to that heritage by turning wine tasting into a spectator sport. We’ve combined elements of the ‘Judgment of Paris’ and the ‘Options Game’, first on our YouTube channel and then at the Bottle Shock Bar, where viewers become competitors. Now we’re ready to take the experience on the road, showing that blind‑tasting wine can be both a spectator and a competitive sport.”

Dates are locked in for the regional cups, although Carter said that the venues are still being finalised.

11 July – Clare Cup

22 August – Adelaide Hills Cup

19 September – Barossa Bowl

24 October – McLaren Vale Cup

14 November – Coonawarra Cup

5 December – Grand Final (Bottle Shock, Gumeracha)

Carter is hoping for the support of venue owners and sponsors to help the project hit the ground running.

“All the biggest breakthroughs in the Australian wine industry have come from collaboration rather than competition, so we’re trying something that’s never been done before. We’ve found a way to educate people through entertainment – by turning learning into a sport. Instead of focusing on consumption, we’re gamifying moderation and showcasing the 85% of the wine experience that rarely gets talked about. The remaining 15% is the alcohol, but that’s not what excites us.

“Wine is really about provenance, place and culture. By gamifying the 85%, we hope venues, sponsors and supporters will help us launch this project. It’s a costly, complex endeavour, but it could bring much‑needed hope and joy to an industry that’s increasingly lacking it,” he emphasised.

For individuals looking to support the competition, Carter said that heading to the website and creating a profile is the easiest way.

“Start by visiting the Bottle Shock Bar in Gumeracha, buying a flight and testing your palate. Then head out into our regions and experience how wine reflects the land – the sight, soil, sunshine and the amazing people behind it.

“That’s how you support the competition: share it with your mates. Everyone knows a friend who thinks they’re a bit of a wine person. Invite them to give it a go and cheer them on. It’s not just about the competition; it’s about having a good time watching your mates win or lose – a classic Australian pastime.”

As for what attendees can expect at the cups, Carter flagged “amazing hospitality, plenty of fun, plenty of laughs, amazing wine and fun challenges”.

“At the core of wine lies hospitality and culture. If it isn’t fun and approachable, we’ve missed the very value wine brings to people’s lives.”

More information and sign-ups can be found here.
